sentences.text: Not [INAUDIBLE].
That we deported us to Germany?
Yes, I think that was beginning of summer.
The months-- I never thought of the months.
It was not a normal train.
There were not trains then.
I think they constructed such quickly for animals.
In Germany, they say feed transport.
That's for animals from place to place to take them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- I remember we traveled for a few days.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were not interested in travel.
It was not a holiday travel that just squeezed in people, a lot of people, all kinds of people.
I ?] never knew them before from all parts of Poland.
No, not at all.
I don't think I've seen it at all.
It was not picturesque to be interested in it.
There were people and people that we had with stony faces.
People didn't talk.
People didn't smile.
They all were afraid and nobody knew where they are going-- where we are going.
Oh, a few hundred.
Yes in a big, big like a truck.
Inside?
No, it couldn't be cold, because so many people, everybody breathing.
It wasn't cold, no.
Not at all.
And no one was interested even to look out.
I don't know if there were windows.
I don't remember windows.
No one looked out and no one was interested.
People talked quietly.
There were some even families with children sent-- whole families were sent.
So, they talked among themselves quietly.
But I wasn't interested in them.
And no one suspected me to be not like one of them.
So, no one was interested.
No one looked at me and I didn't look at them.
I don't remember even drinking or eating.
I don't remember.
I had-- yes, I had what that men gave me in the office, small bread.
So but to drink I don't think we had anything, no.
And I don't know if I ate that bread.
I wasn't hungry.
I didn't feel hungry.
I didn't feel anything.
No.
No, I don't remember.
No one got out or in, because they had to deliver us all.
They had their documents where to let us-- where to the last stop where we would be.
But I remember when we got out.
This I remember.
And it was not the place where I survived, where actually I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was [?
I say ?] American Army.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, it was much earlier.
I don't know was it near.
It was not the same place, not by Meiningen that later I came there.
It was much earlier and somewhere else.
I think it was near Erfurt, a big city.
But we were in a smaller one.
It was like here they call it an employment office, something like this.
It was, I remember when we let us out, then they marched us to a nearby building.
And as we came in, they put us-- they one by one, we had to be in a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like in a queue to stand.
And one of the officials-- I think he was still one who came with us from Poland.
He gave us a yellow-- he put it on.
I don't know how he glued it on whatever we wore-- a square, a yellow square with a-- yes, it was a yellow, quite biggish, a yellow square with a border, a violet border.
And on that yellow square was a big and black P, a P-- Pole for Pole.
Because there were others, too.
There were others from Czechoslovakia.
They all were grouped there.
So, a P is a Pole.
Then they marched us in in a queue.
We had to face into that in a huge [? hole. ?] And there were among us about-- they segregated groups.
We were about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30-- 25, 30.
And we had to stood and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] strange.
As sad I was that I left my child and I knew nothing about my family, I still thought-- I don't know how it came-- I still had a bit of humor in myself.
I thought we stand here like horses one after the other, a queue.
And they would soon come and look into our teeth like horses on a market.
And that how it was.
People on the opposite us stood all the Germans-- men and women, wealthy people from farms, mostly farms.
And they came up one by one and looked at each other.
And I thought, yes, like horses on the market to sell.
They looked at us.
That person who gave us that P left.
He was with the transport.
With us he came.
But the people-- the Germans from opposite-- mostly they were well dressed and well fed from German-- mostly farms.
But they were not peasants.
They were well dressed and well behaving people that had farms, but they had workers on the farms.
They didn't work themselves.
They came up and looked at each other-- on the face, on the hands.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] They looked at those people to see if that particular person will fit into their farm, onto the work would they have-- that he will have to perform.
And they went from one to one.
And they left out me.
They didn't like me.
I wasn't a real made for a farm.
Finally, a very elegant woman-- better type-- and she seemed a very good looking woman.
And I think she was a little bit older than I was, not much.
She was a young person, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] elegantly dressed, smiling, a very beautiful woman.
She came up to me and asked me quietly and said what name is you?
Could you tell me your name?
I said Wanda.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what did you do before?
Did you work?
What did you do before?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

I still was afraid to speak German, and I didn't want-- but in a broken German, because before in Poland if anyone, a Pole, knew German, they suspected that he was Jewish, because Jews-- they all knew that Jews know German, even broken German.
So, I was afraid.
I started to just a few words to say.
I said I on the farm.
Show me your hands.
I showed to him.
She said no.
You didn't work in the farm.
I said yes, in the house.
Maybe, she said.
Do you like children?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, yes, very.
I didn't say that I am a nurse.
I was afraid if I say something I'll give myself out.
I said yes.
I have two children.
Would you like to come visit me to my place?
You didn't go up to anyone else, only to me.
Because they all were peasants.
She wants to have someone better for her house, for her children.
I said yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, she said all right.
She went to the officer.
I don't know if they have to pay something-- got me out.
And I was glad.
Not for ?] [? enough of ?] those things, but I was afraid.
All different people came in and I was afraid maybe someone will recognize me from Lwow or somewhere.
We traveled.
I think it was not very far.
We traveled for about an hour, hour and a half, until we came to her farm.
It was very big.
I think I mentioned before it was more than a farm here.
I can't describe because you can't compare even that her place.
It is called in Germany a [GERMAN].
In Polish, it's [POLISH].
So, we came to her place.
There were fields both sides.
In the middle was a beautiful one story building where she lived with her children.
And she introduced me to her children-- a girl, [?
Rosa Marie. ?] The girl was about-- I don't remember exactly-- five or six years old, seven maybe.
There was a little boy, too, Hans.
And I remember her name was [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she said outside there is a little house for other people who work in the fields with the cows and the sheds, in here, in there.
Everything she had there-- chickens and-- and you will be in my house here.
You will care for the children, nothing else.
Her husband was in the army.
And he was an SS man-- or even Gestapo-- I think in Gestapo.
And he was exactly in Lwow, where I came from.
He was stationed in Lwow.
No.
I don't even remember if he called himself Morhut, like I remember that name.
I don't remember.
He was not home.
He came only for a few days back, not even on holidays.
I don't know-- whenever he was on leave.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so, as I said before, I stayed for a while there.
And I think she rather liked me.
If she suspected-- I think she did.
I think she did, because finally slowly, slowly, I started to speak a little bit.
She never asked, never questioned me-- asked.
Because the Poles were-- there were about 12 or 15 Poles with families and even with children, a son of about 20, a couple and their little daughter of about 10.
And they stayed there, not far from her building.
It was another specialty building for the people working.
So, they all knew that I had better conditions there.
I was in the house.
And they were in that house, that little one especially built for-- well, they had it before for workers for many workers, yes.
And they used to go [? every-- ?] [? there ?] was not far from it.
It was not far from Meiningen.
The biggest city was Meiningen, although not a very big city.
And these people used to walk to-- not far from Meiningen-- to a church every Sunday.
And they called me, because I said Polish girls.
Then I had to go to the church.
And she let me go.
If she understood or not, first, I don't know.
I think she suspected something.
She was a very well-educated person and quite clever.
I think she suspected.
And she said, Wanda, if you want to go to church with other people, you can go.
So, I went with them.
I had even photos from the church there and the priests there.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know where I have it.
I had it for a while.
Maybe somewhere it's still there.
And he-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] amongst these people there-- no, he was not with us.
Because people from different nearby places where they worked-- or farms to say-- used to come to us, because we had the most people.
And there were some who employed only two or three people, because that person that I stayed with, she had the largest farm.
As I said, smaller ones had less people of those sent from all countries around.
They never-- they didn't pay us for work, never, nothing.
They didn't pay.
They didn't even-- those Germans didn't even feed us.
We were not well fed, not at all.
I had at least-- I mean, she liked me, but she didn't give me much.
Germans are known for it.
She gave me a piece of bread for breakfast and a bit of coffee, very little.
And the people there had even less.
They didn't care about them at all.
They didn't give them food.
As they cooked, I remember on huge, big-- how do you call it?
I don't know.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Like iron big.

I don't know how they call it where they cooked in it on the fire-- potatoes, little potatoes, not even the good ones that they selected for sale but tiny little they cooked for pigs in these big-- I don't know how they call it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Urns, like urns something, huge ones.
So, you cooked fire underneath, outside on the farm.
They cooked potatoes.
And these people took these potatoes out and they ate them.
When they gave a pig, they ate it.
They didn't feed them.
They didn't give them.
And they were not paid.
But what they could get themselves out in fields and cook for themselves, that they were allowed, like carrots they got out on the fields and cooked for themselves, potatoes.
On that they lived for a few years.
So, I went with them to the church.
There was one Ukrainian, a big man.
He was about-- he could be about 30 or 35.
He came from another farm.
They congregated together, but it was not allowed.
The Gestapo came every day.
They didn't allowed people to get together, only to stay where they worked.
So, every day came Gestapo there.
But Gestapo left as they came.
That man, he was Ukrainian.
He said that Wanda, she has-- he said in Ukrainian [NON-ENGLISH] is Russian-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- a Jewish head or a Jewish mind.
She looks to me like that.
So, there was another one-- a Ukrainian-- in our group.
And he just wanted to be my boyfriend, because I appealed him because I was-- they called me [NON-ENGLISH].
It means a city woman.
I looked better one than the others, so he wanted to be my boyfriend.
I said to him, look.
Can you hear what he says?
He was the-- that was a very funny story.
He was the worst.
He go with all other people if they said something he didn't like, he just got out a knife-- a knife.
I selected him to be my boyfriend, not another.
People wonder why did I take the worst one?
He was like a criminal there.
He was beaten by the Gestapo a hundred times, because he did something that they didn't like.
He was a rebel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Always on knives, so I said-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name, I remember.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH] did he hear-- did you hear what he said?
We went up to him.
If I hear once more that you say to my Wanda she is Jewish, I cut you in half and took out the knife.
The other one was scared, no more.
Later that woman she asked me, Wanda, tell me.
Couldn't you find for a boyfriend a better one?
There are nice boys on the other farm.
[NON-ENGLISH] has a very nice Pole.
I said-- I couldn't tell her the story why I selected him, because I knew I would be safe with him.
I said I like him.
That was all finished.
He went-- when we were liberated later, much later he went after me when we went back before-- I didn't tell you-- after the war.
It was after we already-- he was still behind me.
And once, it was in Bamberg, there were only Jews.
I didn't tell you later.
It will come.
That we started from Bamberg in Germany before I came to near Nuremberg I worked.
After then, he was behind me.
And I told him all the time, look.
I had a boyfriend in Poland, so I can't take you as really as my boyfriend because if he'll find out.
So, he said all right.
Maybe he's dead?
I said all right.
Maybe he's dead.
He went after me.
And when we came in Bamberg, I knew we were all Jewish people after the liberation.
I told them, look.
Get away him.
He's after me all the time and he helped me in Germany.
They went up to him and he said-- they told him, [NON-ENGLISH].
Is your name [NON-ENGLISH]?
Wanda is Jewish.
Do you know that?
He said [INAUDIBLE].
He never knew.
[INAUDIBLE] in Germany [INAUDIBLE].
How long was that till the liberation? '45.
She's nuts.
It's not true.
She's not Jewish.
He said, yes, ask her.
He came up to me.
He said, Wanda, tell me what they are talking.
I said, yes, I am Jewish, of course.
You never knew that?
I said that meant that he told one-- that Wanda has a Jewish [? hat. ?] He knew.
And you said you will cut him in half if he does something to your Wanda.
[LAUGHS] I had to save myself, but I did.
How long did you stay with Ilsa Morhut?
Pardon?
With that family?
Till the liberation, till '45.
And she saved me and I told you how.
It was at the hospital [INAUDIBLE].
Pardon?
Can I tell it again?
After I've been with her about three weeks and I know that she liked me, she was actually against her husband.
She was not at all Nazi, because Germany was divided.
There were many-- you know there was attempt on Hitler.
It was in May-- what year was it-- an attempt to kill him.
There were-- in the army, there were some against him, what-- and people knew what he is doing.
At the beginning, it was killing in the concentration camps.
He tried to hide everything, but the army knew.
They found out, and some were against him.
People say all Germany.
It's not so at all.
I know it, because I've been there and I've seen it.
And she was against her husband.
They never had a fight, but he used to come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not too often he came home, and he had to leave for about a week or two.
Once he came home, and he told her that he is going to leave her.
And she was very much in love with him.
He was a good looking man, but he was in the Gestapo.
And he came home all-- another story I'll tell later-- all decorated.
But later on, I know I felt that she told him.
And he never did anything against me.
She told him in a way.
How, I don't know.
I just suspected.
I don't know for sure that when he told her that he's going to leave her, that he has an Ukrainian girl there, in Ukraine.
It means Lwow was Ukraine once.
It's now Ukraine, too.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was promised a bigger farm there in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland-- Ukraine.
Eastern Poland is called Ukraine.
So, she wanted to commit suicide when she heard that he's going to leave her for good-- with two children, of course.
So, but I noticed something at night time.
I went the room-- the two children had their room just a door through both rooms near her, and I was a room further.
So, during the night, I went in to see how the two, how they're covered or if they sleep.
Well, and as the door was open to their mother's room, I noticed something unusual, that she was sleeping and her arm hanging from bed.
So, straight away I went in and I noticed scattered on the floor tablets and a bottle standing nearby.
I understood that she wanted to commit suicide.
So, I just-- being a nurse, I knew what to do.
I went down-- downstairs was a big kitchen.
I warmed up some milk and I just poured it into her.
She was half dead.
If I wouldn't notice, I think she would die.
And she started to cough and brought out the tablets.
It means that it happened not long before, because the tablets were still not digested, fell out of her.
And her husband was then on leave at home, but not with her.
Over the whole room was opposite, and he slept there.
I knocked on his door, and not before.
I was afraid that maybe he won't help her.
But when I brought her back to life, she started to breathe.
And she couldn't talk.
She was still very pale, I remember.
But I set her up and knocked quick.
I wanted to call a doctor.
I didn't know where, and I don't know if there were some doctors during the war.
So, I knocked on his door, and he came in and saw what happened.
So, then he rang a doctor and a doctor came.
He said nothing more to do.
You helped her quite well.
If all the tablets are out and she started to breathe again, it means that I can't do anything else.
She will come to herself and she will be all right.
And he left.
The doctor didn't do much.
Nothing was there to do.
And she got better, of course, and got up in the morning.
She was all right.
And he-- but a few days later, he left.
And I haven't seen him anymore.
He didn't come back.
But then she told me that she got news that he-- it was bombarded.
He was sent from Lwow to Berlin.
And in Berlin he just was somewhere in a building.
It was bombarded and he died, her husband.
